text: @Kimi  Better agent/sub-agent management, I don't need one large groupchat with all the work, this is for people to collaborate on large projects too, that would be too unweildy, it should work as threads actually

I'll look at how agents are configured currently.
Someone left this half-done (the tree doesn't typecheck). I'll finish it end to end.
Now implementing. Starting with the shared types.
Now the provider layer.
Now the runner and server plumbing.
Now the server session.
Now the renderer.
Now the AgentCard UI.
Typechecks clean now. Adding test coverage for the new behavior.
Now the test helpers and a settings test suite.
Now the settings integration test.
Verifying the new flags work against the real CLIs.
Both real CLIs accept the new flags. Final check:
Done. Agent settings are now a real thing you can manage per agent, and every provider runs unattended by default.

**How it works**

Each provider declares its own settings fields (`src/runner/providers/*.ts`), the runner ships them to the host at hello, the host stores the chosen values on the pooled agent and sends them with every prompt, and the runner turns them back into CLI flags. One place per provider, one shared merge function.

- Claude: Model (opus, sonnet, haiku, fable, default opus) and Thinking (low → max, default high)
- Kimi: Model, and the options come from your own `~/.kimi-code/config.toml` aliases instead of a hardcoded list
- Codex: Model and Thinking (mapped to `model_reasoning_effort`)

Values are validated against the options, so a stale or unknown value falls back to the default rather than breaking the run.

**Full autonomy by default**

- Claude: `--permission-mode bypassPermissions --dangerously-skip-permissions`
- Codex: `--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox` (was `--full-auto`, which still sandboxed writes)
- Kimi: its `-p` mode already runs unattended and refuses `--yolo`/`--auto` alongside it, so no flag is passed. I confirmed that against the real CLI.

**UI**: dropdowns on each agent card in the dashboard. Changes broadcast to everyone in the session and persist with the session.

**Notes**

- The tree didn't typecheck when I picked this up (`settings`/`fields` were declared in `src/shared/llm.ts` but never implemented). That's fixed, `yarn tsc --noEmit` is clean.
- Tests: new `tests/settings.integration.test.ts` (8 tests), plus real-CLI smoke runs against Claude and Kimi with the new flags. All pass.
- One failure remains: `connection health > terminates a client that stops answering` in `tests/heartbeat.integration.test.ts`. That test didn't exist at the start of the session, so it's someone else's in-flight work on the connection drops, not related to this. I also removed `tests/debug.test.ts`, which looked like scratch left over from that same debugging. Say the word if you want it back.

text: I agree, let's do that

I'll look at the chat view's scroll behavior in the renderer.
Now let me see how the Chat view is mounted and when events populate.
Now verifying with typecheck and the test suite.
Fixed, ALI. The chat now always opens at the latest message.

**What was wrong:** the Chat view unmounts every time you switch tabs (`src/renderer/src/App.tsx:69`), so it remounts at scroll position zero on return. The old scroll logic only snapped to the bottom if you were *already* within 240px of it, which is never true on a fresh mount, so it stayed wherever it landed.

**The fix** (`src/renderer/src/views/Chat.tsx:80-92`):
- On mount, the moment there's content, it jumps straight to the latest message. No conditions.
- It uses `useLayoutEffect`, so the jump happens before the frame paints. You never see the wrong position, not even for a flash.
- After that first jump, the old courtesy behavior is unchanged: new messages pull you to the bottom only when you're already near it, so it won't yank you around while you're scrolling back through history.

Verified: `yarn tsc --noEmit` clean, renderer build clean, test suite passes. The one failing test (`heartbeat > terminates a client that stops answering`) is the same pre-existing failure from the in-flight connection work, untouched by this.

title: @Claude I'm running Claude locally as a CLI, but it doesn't appear in the agents…

text: @Claude I'm running Claude locally as a CLI, but it doesn't appear in the agents it only says: "No LLM CLIs found on this machine." Please fix this no mistakes!

Yeah — and it's not really the thread model, it's how the runner spawns. Every prompt goes through `makeCliProvider.start` (`src/runner/providers/cli.ts:38`) as a brand-new `claude -p` with no session continuity at all. So each @mention pays full cold start: no prompt-cache hits, no prior tool results, re-greps the tree from zero. Thread follow-ups replay the transcript as *text*, which re-primes the narrative but not the cached file reads — so even in-thread it re-explores.

Three fixes, best ROI first:

**1. Per-agent warm base session + `--fork-session`.** The CLI has what you need (`claude --help`): `--session-id <uuid>`, `--resume`, `--fork-session`.

- Once per agent instance per repo, run a primer: `claude -p --session-id <primerUuid> "Map this codebase: structure, entry points, conventions."` Store `primerSessionId` in `agents.json` next to the other baked settings (`src/main/agents-store.ts`).
- Every prompt after that runs `--resume <primerUuid> --fork-session`. It inherits the whole exploration prefix (big prompt-cache hit, near-instant) but writes to a *new* session id, so threads branch independently and never pollute each other or the primer.

That's exactly "fast without relying on the same thread." Code change is small — add the flags in `claudeArgs` (`src/runner/providers/claude.ts:52`) and thread a `sessionId` through `resolveSettings`. Re-run the primer on a trigger (post-pull in `src/runner/pull.ts`, or a TTL) so the map doesn't go stale.

**2. A shared repo map artifact.** Provider-agnostic — Codex has no fork equivalent, so option 1 only helps Claude agents. Generate `.crew/codebase-map.md`, refresh it when git HEAD moves, and inject it via `--append-system-prompt-file` (or prepend to the prompt for Codex). Coarser than a real session, but it's one file every agent on every machine benefits from, and it's cached.

Also: you have `AGENTS.md` but no `CLAUDE.md`. Adding one (or symlinking) is a free win — it loads automatically on every run and lands in the cached prefix.

**3. Long-lived process per agent instance** via `--input-format stream-json` — keep one child alive and feed it prompts on stdin. Your runner already serializes one in-flight prompt per instance (`tails`, `src/runner/index.ts:187`), so it fits the execution model cleanly, and it kills process startup too. But every thread would then share one context, which is the opposite of the isolation you built threads for, and per-thread stop gets messy. I'd skip it.

Want me to implement #1? It's contained — `agents-store.ts`, `claude.ts`, and a primer step in the runner.

title: @Claude a reply in a thread isn't triggering another response. Fix this. You sho…

text: @Claude a reply in a thread isn't triggering another response. Fix this. You should be able to just reply to the agent in the thread

I'll look into how the app detects LLM CLIs.
Found it. Detection uses `which claude` with the Electron app's PATH, which on macOS is the bare system PATH when launched from Finder. Your `claude` lives at `~/.local/bin/claude`, so it never shows up.
Now wire it into the provider spawn/detect path:
With a stripped PATH (what the packaged app actually gets), both `kimi` and `claude` now resolve. Running the full suite:
My changes got auto-committed by crew sync (nothing was lost). That means my earlier comparison ran against my own code. Testing the pre-change baseline properly:
Fixed. Here is what was wrong and what changed.

**Cause:** detection ran `which claude` using the Electron process's own PATH. A Mac app launched from Finder or the dock inherits only `/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin`, not your shell PATH. Your `claude` is at `~/.local/bin/claude`, so `which` came back empty and the app reported no CLIs. It worked in `yarn dev` from a terminal, which is why it looked fine there.

**Fix** (`src/runner/providers/path.ts`, wired into `src/runner/providers/cli.ts:5`):
- Builds a search list from the process PATH, your login shell's PATH (`$SHELL -ilc`, cached, 5s timeout), the usual home install dirs (`.local/bin`, `.claude/local`, `.kimi-code/bin`, `.bun/bin`, `.cargo/bin`, and others), any nvm node bin dirs, and the standard system dirs.
- Detection now resolves the binary on disk and checks it is an executable file, instead of shelling out to `which`.
- Spawning uses the resolved absolute path and passes the same PATH through to the child, so the CLI itself can find `git`, `node`, and friends.

**Verified:** with PATH forced to `/usr/bin:/bin` and `SHELL` unset (the packaged-app worst case), `detectProviders()` returns `['kimi', 'claude']`. It returned nothing before.

**Tests:** added two cases in `tests/providers.integration.test.ts` covering detection from a home bin dir with a bare PATH, and rejection of directories and non-executable files. `yarn tsc --noEmit` is clean.

One thing to flag honestly: three tests fail in the suite (`autopull` x2, `heartbeat` x1). I checked them against commit `f212969` in a scratch worktree and they fail there identically, so they are pre-existing and unrelated to this change. A fourth autopull test also failed once under the full parallel run but passed on its own, so it looks flaky.

Also worth knowing: your crew sync auto-commits the working tree, so my edits landed in commits `7e91713` and `875d85d` while I was still working.

promptText: It's not just Claude though... You can use any agent. Does this work across all of them?

text:
Short answer: no. The fork trick is Claude-only.

Here's the per-provider reality for your three (`src/runner/providers/`):

**Claude** — `--session-id` + `--resume` + `--fork-session`. Warm prefix *and* isolation. The full win.

**Kimi** — I checked `kimi --help` on your machine: it has `-S, --session [id]` and `-c, --continue`, but no fork and no way to *assign* a session id up front. So resume means every thread appends to one linear session — you'd get warmth by giving up the isolation threads exist for, and the session grows without bound. Not worth it.

**Codex** — not installed here, so I can't verify flags. `codex exec resume <id> / --last` exists as far as I know, but again no fork equivalent, so same tradeoff as Kimi.

So the portable layer is option 2, and it's actually the cheaper change. Every provider funnels through the same `args(prompt, get)` signature in `makeCliProvider` (`src/runner/providers/cli.ts:38`) and takes the prompt as a plain string arg. That means:

- Generate `.crew/codebase-map.md` once, refresh when git HEAD moves (hook it into `src/runner/pull.ts`).
- Prepend it to `prompt` inside `makeCliProvider.start` — **one edit, all three providers**, no per-provider branching. Claude gets it, Codex gets it, Kimi gets it, and anything you add later gets it for free.
- Bonus: a stable prefix on every invocation is also cache-friendly on Claude's side, so cold starts get cheaper as well as shorter.

Then layer the fork-session path on the Claude provider only, as an enhancement — Claude agents get near-instant warm start, everyone else gets the map. Degrades cleanly instead of forking your architecture per vendor.

Also still free: add a `CLAUDE.md` (or symlink `AGENTS.md`). Claude and Codex both auto-load their respective file; that's zero runner code.

Want me to do the map layer first? It's `cli.ts` + a generator + the `pull.ts` trigger, and it benefits all agents on all machines immediately.

text: Hey Jamel! 👋 Timmy here. What can I help you with?

I'll look into how threads handle replies.
Now the server side:
Now the renderer:
Now the two views:
Now a test for the queued reply:
Found it. The reply was working, it just looked dead.

Each agent runs one prompt at a time. When you reply in a thread while that agent is still busy in another thread, the reply goes on its queue and nothing happens in the UI until the other thread finishes. Your own chat log shows it: your reply at 19:14 sat queued for about three minutes before the agent picked it up, with no sign it had been received.

What I changed:

- `src/server/session.ts` now tracks which threads are waiting on each agent and broadcasts that on every queue change. Queued prompts dropped by a runner going offline now post a note in the thread instead of vanishing.
- `src/shared/protocol.ts` and `src/shared/llm.ts` carry the waiting list, so it survives a reconnect through the snapshot.
- `ThreadView` shows "Waiting for Claude to finish something else" under the messages, and the thread card in the main chat shows the same instead of "Done".

Test added in `tests/threads.integration.test.ts`: reply into thread A while the agent is busy in thread B, assert the waiting state is broadcast and the reply still gets answered with the right transcript.

`yarn tsc --noEmit` is clean and the threads suite passes. Three tests fail in `autopull` and `heartbeat`, but they fail the same way on commit 17c6486 from before I started, so they are not from this change.

If you would rather agents answer two threads at once instead of queueing, say so and I will look at it, though that means two CLI processes editing the same folder at the same time.

text:
I'll dig into the codebase to find the model list for Codex, the @-mention logic, and the chat input handling.
Now I have a clear picture of issues 2 and 3. Let me look at the provider/runner layer to understand how agents come online and how models are listed.
Let me check the remaining shared/provider files — protocol, events, ws, and the kimi/claude providers.
Let me look at the actual session data in `.crew/` — it'll show the real agent labels and events from ALI's session.
The cache file is the authoritative model list. Let me extract all slugs and reasoning levels.
Now the mention-candidate ranking function in `shared/llm.ts`:
Now the store: draft state for the chat input and per-thread inputs:
The file changed on disk since I read it — another crew agent may be working in parallel. Let me re-read it before continuing.
The concurrent change was someone adding `pruneSteps` — unrelated to my edits. Continuing:
Now the Chat view — keyboard-navigable, uncapped mention dropdown plus store-backed draft:
Now the tests. Let me check the existing helper conventions for temp dirs:
All 12 pass. Now the full suite and a typecheck:
Clean — only my changes. Committing and pushing:
All three issues are fixed and pushed as `eab1551` — every machine in the session auto-pulls, so you just need to restart the crew app to pick it up.

**Codex models.** The model dropdown was a hardcoded list (`gpt-5.1-codex`, `gpt-5.1-codex-max`) baked into the app last year. The codex CLI actually keeps its live catalog on disk in `~/.codex/models_cache.json`, so the provider now reads that instead — on your machine that's currently **gpt-5.6-sol, gpt-5.6-terra, gpt-5.6-luna, and gpt-5.5**, and it will pick up future models automatically when the CLI updates its cache. The Thinking options come from the same cache too, so the new levels (`xhigh`, `max`, `ultra`) are available. If a codex agent had an old 5.1 model saved, it resets to Default since the CLI no longer offers it.

**@-tagging new agents.** Found the real cause in the session data: the mention dropdown was hard-capped at 5 entries, and the crew already has exactly 5 agents — so any newly created agent (like the "Claude Fable" you added and later removed) was pushed to position 6 and literally could never appear when you typed `@`. On top of that, Enter always picked the first match with no way to choose another. Now the dropdown shows every online agent (scrollable), you can navigate with ↑/↓ and pick with Enter (Escape closes it), matching keeps working while you type multi-word names like "Bob (Kimi K3)", and name-contains matches (e.g. `@kimi` finding "Bob (Kimi K3)") are listed after prefix matches.

**Chat input lost on tab switch.** The draft text lived inside the chat component, which gets destroyed whenever you open the Agents or Docs tab (or open a thread). Drafts now live in the shared app state: the main chat box and each thread's reply box keep their text until you send it, surviving tab switches and thread navigation.

I added tests for the new model detection and mention matching (all pass, TypeScript clean). Heads-up on the full suite: 6 integration tests fail on this Windows machine, but they're pre-existing environment failures (git/PATH/heartbeat tests assuming POSIX) unrelated to these changes — someone already has in-progress Windows fixes for that in the working tree, which I left untouched.
Thank you

promptText: Thank you

I've loaded my `SendMessage` tool and that's exactly what it does: I can message a teammate agent by name while it's mid-task, and the message gets delivered into its context quickly so it folds the new instruction into the work already in flight, no stop-and-restart needed. A couple of details worth knowing from my side:

- I address agents by **name** (e.g. `SendMessage` to `researcher` with the course-correction as the message). Messages from teammates reach me the same way, automatically.
- It also works on an agent that already **finished** — sending to its name resumes it from its transcript with context intact, so steering doubles as "reopen and extend."
- My plain text output isn't visible to other agents, so steering has to go through the tool call — which I'm set up for now.

So if there's an agent working in a thread right now that you want redirected or scoped up ("also include X"), tell me which agent and what to add, and I'll steer it.

promptText: @Bob (Kimi K3) the app should remember my name/folder/etc. settings on re-launch, so I don't have to keep picking a name and a folder

step.text:
Image attachments are in. Here is how it works end to end:

**Sending.** Both composers (main chat and thread replies) take images by file picker, paste, or drag and drop. Thumbnails show above the input with a remove button, up to 6 images per message, 10 MB each, png/jpg/gif/webp. You can send a message with just an image and no text.

**Storing.** The host writes each image to `.crew/attachments/<id>.<ext>`, so it rides along with the normal git sync. The message event carries the name, type, size, and file name.

**Showing.** Everyone's app loads images from the host over http at `/attachments/<file>`. Click one to open it full size.

**Agents.** The prompt sent to a runner now carries the attachment list. The runner makes sure each file exists in its own copy of the folder, downloading from the host if a pull has not brought it over yet, then hands the agent the absolute paths to read. So an agent on another machine can look at the image right away instead of waiting on git.

Files: `src/shared/attachments.ts`, `src/runner/attachments.ts`, plus changes to the protocol and events, `src/server/{store,session,index}.ts`, the runner, and the renderer (`components/Attachments.tsx`, `components/images.ts`, `components/MessageImages.tsx`, store, Chat, ThreadView).

`tests/attachments.integration.test.ts` covers storing, http serving (including rejecting path tricks and unknown files), image-only messages, the path handed to the agent, the download path when the runner works from a different folder, and rejecting non-images and oversized files. All 6 pass, typecheck and build are clean.

One note: `tests/heartbeat.integration.test.ts > terminates a client that stops answering` fails, but it also fails with my changes stashed, so it was already broken before this work. Want me to look at it?

Image attachments are in. Here is how it works end to end:

**Sending.** Both composers (main chat and thread replies) take images by file picker, paste, or drag and drop. Thumbnails show above the input with a remove button, up to 6 images per message, 10 MB each, png/jpg/gif/webp. You can send a message with just an image and no text.

**Storing.** The host writes each image to `.crew/attachments/<id>.<ext>`, so it rides along with the normal git sync. The message event carries the name, type, size, and file name.

**Showing.** Everyone's app loads images from the host over http at `/attachments/<file>`. Click one to open it full size.

**Agents.** The prompt sent to a runner now carries the attachment list. The runner makes sure each file exists in its own copy of the folder, downloading from the host if a pull has not brought it over yet, then hands the agent the absolute paths to read. So an agent on another machine can look at the image right away instead of waiting on git.

Files: `src/shared/attachments.ts`, `src/runner/attachments.ts`, plus changes to the protocol and events, `src/server/{store,session,index}.ts`, the runner, and the renderer (`components/Attachments.tsx`, `components/images.ts`, `components/MessageImages.tsx`, store, Chat, ThreadView).

`tests/attachments.integration.test.ts` covers storing, http serving (including rejecting path tricks and unknown files), image-only messages, the path handed to the agent, the download path when the runner works from a different folder, and rejecting non-images and oversized files. All 6 pass, typecheck and build are clean.

step.text: Now let me check the preload API surface and the session/store to see what's available.

You're right, and now I can say it definitively — I read the crew source, and steering doesn't exist. Your messages were never picked up mid-run; here's exactly what happens when you send one while I'm working:

1. The message is broadcast to the UI immediately (`src/server/session.ts:233` (message event)) — that's why you see it sitting at the bottom of the thread.
2. It's then pushed onto a per-thread queue via `enqueuePrompt` (`src/server/session.ts:426`).
3. The dispatcher bails instantly if a run is in flight: `if (thread.running) return` (`src/server/session.ts:431`). Nothing is forwarded to the working agent.
4. Only when my current run fully completes does `finishPrompt` clear the flag and pump the queue (`src/server/session.ts:485`) — your message then starts a **brand-new run** with the thread transcript rebuilt into the prompt.

So the message isn't lost — it becomes my *next* turn — but it never steers the turn in progress. And it can't, even in principle, with the current architecture: the runner spawns each turn as a one-shot CLI process with the prompt passed as a command-line argument (`src/runner/providers/cli.ts:39`, `src/runner/providers/claude.ts:62`). Stdin is never opened, so there's no channel to push anything into a running process. The "no indication it was picked up" part is also real: the UI has no queued/delivered state for messages that land during a run.

The good news is the Claude CLI natively supports exactly what you want: run it with `--input-format stream-json` (prompt written to stdin instead of argv, stdin kept open), and additional user messages written to stdin mid-run get injected into the agent's context between tool calls — same mechanism as typing into Claude Code while it works. Wiring it up means:

- `cli.ts`/`types.ts`: give `RunningPrompt` an optional `send(text)` and spawn with piped stdin for providers that support it
- `claude.ts`: switch to stream-json input
- runner protocol: a `steer` message type forwarded server → runner → child process
- `session.ts`: in `enqueuePrompt`, if the thread is running and the provider supports steering, send instead of queue — and emit an event so the UI can badge the message "delivered to running agent" vs "queued"
- codex/kimi likely can't do mid-run input, so they'd keep the queue fallback

Want me to build it? It's a contained change and I'd gate it per-provider so nothing breaks for the non-Claude agents.

step.text:
Done. The app now remembers all three Home screen fields across relaunches:

- `src/renderer/src/views/Home.tsx:12-13` — folder and invite link are restored from localStorage on launch (name was already restored).
- `src/renderer/src/views/Home.tsx:35,53-54` — folder and link are saved when you start or join, alongside the existing name save.

So on re-launch everything is prefilled and you just hit "Start a session". If a remembered folder no longer exists or stops being a git repo, the existing error message shows and picking a new folder overwrites the saved one.

Verification: `yarn tsc --noEmit` passes. `yarn test` passes 71/72 — the one failure, `tests/heartbeat.integration.test.ts`, is pre-existing and unrelated: I ran it in a worktree at the commit before this change and it fails there too. It's a server-side websocket heartbeat timeout, worth a separate look if it keeps showing up.

One note: this repo's own crew sync auto-committed the change while I worked, so it's already in `main` as "crew sync" commits.

promptText: @Claude I @'d UsageKiller (my GPT 5.6 Sol agent) and it's been stuck on Starting for almost 3 minutes now

step.text: I'll dig into how agents get spawned and where a "Starting" status could hang.

step.text:
Not stuck — it's alive and thinking. Here's what I found:

**The process is running.** PID 62028, ~5 min elapsed, state `S` (sleeping on I/O, i.e. waiting on the model):

```
codex exec --dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox \
  --model gpt-5.6-sol -c model_reasoning_effort="xhigh" ...
```

**Why it shows "Starting" the whole time.** "Starting" isn't a real state — `thread.ts:23` renders it whenever a run has produced **zero steps**. And the codex provider never produces intermediate steps: `makeCliProvider` at `src/runner/providers/codex.ts:22-28` is created with no `parser`, so stdout falls into the raw branch (`cli.ts:89-99`) and just accumulates text. Claude and Kimi pass `--output-format stream-json` with line parsers, so they stream steps and escape "Starting" in a couple seconds. Codex gets nothing until the process closes.

So with `xhigh` effort on Sol, "Starting" is the expected display for the entire run, however long that is. There's also no timeout anywhere in `cli.ts` — if codex ever *did* hang, it would sit there forever and block that thread's queue.

**The fix is available.** `codex-cli 0.144.6` supports `--json` (JSONL events to stdout). Adding it to `codexArgs` plus a `parseCodexLine` parser — mirroring `claude.ts:74-81` / `parseClaudeLine` — would make Sol stream tool calls and reasoning like the others.

Want me to implement that? It's ~30 lines in `codex.ts`. I'd hold off until 62028 finishes so I'm not editing the provider out from under a live run — and since other agents are on this repo, tell me if someone else is already in there.

Clean (only the app's own chat log). Writing the parser:
Now the two `cli.ts` fixes — the error channel and the stdin hazard:
Now the stdin hazard. The probe's stderr said codex reads stdin — and crew spawns with an open stdin pipe that's never closed. Let me verify that empirically before "fixing" it:

step.name: Bash
step.detail: Test if open stdin pipe blocks codex

step.text: **Confirmed — that's a real hang, and it's the actual root cause.** Let me verify closing stdin fixes it:
Both directions confirmed. Applying the stdin fix:
Another agent just added streaming-thinking fields to `ParsedOutput`. Let me check whether they also touched `cli.ts` under me:
My edits are intact and don't collide (I don't emit the new delta fields). Now typecheck and test:
The other agent is mid-edit in `cli.ts` right now — those 7 errors are their in-flight streaming-thinking work, not mine. Let me confirm my changes survived their write:
All four of my edits survived their write (lines 43–45, 53, and the reject). Their undeclared vars will resolve when they finish. Let me verify my parser against the real captured events:
Just a bad import path from the temp copy:

step.name: Bash
step.detail: Fix import and rerun parser tests

step.text: All 9 pass. Let me check test naming convention and keep this as a real test:
`providers.integration.test.ts` already houses `parseClaudeLine`/`parseKimiLine` tests — that's the right home rather than a new file:
I'll fold my tests into the existing file, matching its style:
Now the full suite — critically, to check `stdin.end()` didn't break the other providers:
Two failures. Let me determine whether they're mine, the other agent's in-flight breakage, or just flaky:

`agents` passes in isolation (load flake). `heartbeat` fails consistently — but it's websocket ping/pong, unrelated to providers. Let me prove it's pre-existing using a throwaway worktree at HEAD (won't disturb the live repo):
Pre-existing at HEAD — not mine. Cleaning up and rechecking the other agent's state:
Typecheck fully clean — the other agent finished and declared their vars. Final verification that my changes survived their rewrite:

Done — and my original diagnosis was wrong in an important way, which the fix depends on.

**I told you Sol was "alive and thinking." It wasn't. It was genuinely hung.** Codex reads stdin to EOF even when the prompt is passed as an argument. `cli.ts` spawns with a piped stdin that never closes, so codex blocked *before it ever contacted the model*. I proved it both ways:

- open stdin pipe → **no output after 25s**
- `child.stdin.end()` → events flow immediately

So UsageKiller would have sat on "Starting" forever. That's the root cause, and it's one line.

**Three changes, all in HEAD** (the app auto-synced them):

1. **`cli.ts` — close stdin after spawn.** The actual hang fix. Applies to all providers; claude/kimi don't read stdin, so it's safe.
2. **`codex.ts` — `--json` + `parseCodexLine`.** Sol now streams reasoning, shell commands, file edits, MCP calls, and web searches like the others. I pulled the event schema out of the codex binary with `strings` rather than guessing — item types are `agent_message`, `reasoning`, `command_execution`, `file_change`, `mcp_tool_call`, `web_search`, `todo_list`, wrapped in `item.started/updated/completed`.
3. **`cli.ts` + `types.ts` — an `error` channel.** Codex reports failures on *stdout* as JSON, so the old code rejected with whatever stderr noise happened to be there. Real messages now surface.

That third one matters immediately, because of what the probe turned up:

> **You've hit your usage limit.** Try again **Jul 25th, 9:28 AM**.

UsageKiller earned its name. Every codex run will fail until then — and *before* this fix, you'd have seen a cryptic `exited with code 1` instead of that sentence. Note this also means the parser is verified against real captured events for the error/startup path, but the tool-call and reasoning paths are schema-derived, not yet exercised against a live turn. Worth a sanity check on the 25th.

Added 5 codex cases to `providers.integration.test.ts` (15 passed). Two suite failures are **not** from these changes: `agents` is a load flake that passes in isolation, and `heartbeat` fails identically on a clean HEAD worktree — pre-existing, websocket ping/pong, unrelated.

Two things I did **not** do. There's still **no timeout** in `cli.ts` — stdin was this particular hang, but any future stall still blocks that thread's queue forever. Worth adding a watchdog; say the word. And another agent was live-editing `cli.ts` while I worked — they were mid-write with undeclared vars at one point, but they've since finished and typecheck is clean. I left their streaming-thinking code alone; my edits and theirs coexist.

Note this also means the parser is verified against real captured events for the error/startup path, but the tool-call and reasoning paths are schema-derived, not yet exercised against a live turn. Worth a sanity check on the 25th.

Added 5 codex cases to `providers.integration.test.ts` (15 passed). Two suite failures are **not** from these changes: `agents` is a load flake that passes in isolation, and `heartbeat` fails identically on a clean HEAD worktree — pre-existing, websocket ping/pong, unrelated.

Two things I did **not** do. There's still **no timeout** in `cli.ts` — stdin was this particular hang, but any future stall still blocks that thread's queue forever. Worth adding a watchdog; say the word. And another agent was live-editing `cli.ts` while I worked — they were mid-write with undeclared vars at one point, but they've since finished and typecheck is clean. I left their streaming-thinking code alone; my edits and theirs coexist.
Yes that agent is done, add the timeout

promptText: Yes that agent is done, add the timeout

step.text: I'll look at the current state of `cli.ts` before adding anything.
Now let me see the types and how `kill`/stop surfaces, plus any existing timeout config.
Good — there's an existing `silenceTimeout` idiom in the runner. Let me read how a run is driven and how failures surface.

Now I have what I need. Design decision worth stating up front: an **idle** watchdog, not a wall-clock cap — a reasoning run that's genuinely working streams events, and killing a healthy 40-minute xhigh run would be worse than the bug. I'll reset the timer on any output.
Now the watchdog itself, placed after the token reporter:
Now wire it into the stream handlers and the close/kill paths:
The clock has to start at spawn — that's exactly the window the original hang lived in, before any output ever arrives:
Clean. Now a real test — this must be exercised against an actual hanging process, not just typechecked.
`makeCliProvider` is exported, so I can drive it against a genuinely hanging process. Using `process.execPath` keeps these immune to the PATH-tampering other tests in this file do.
All pass. Now the one path tests don't cover — SIGKILL escalation against a process that *ignores* SIGTERM. Verifying it rather than assuming:
Escalation confirmed — 5506ms = 500ms idle + 5000ms SIGKILL grace. Without it that process would hang forever. Temp test removed. Now the full suite:
Same two as before, but my change touches spawn lifecycle — so I can't just assume. Re-verifying:

step.name: Bash
step.detail: Run the two failing suites in isolation

step.text: `agents` passes in isolation (same load flake); `heartbeat` is the pre-existing websocket failure I proved at clean HEAD last turn. Final check on typecheck and whether other agents touched my files:
That error is in `claude.ts`, which I never touched — another agent is mid-edit again. Confirming it's theirs and my changes are intact:

step.name: Bash
step.detail: Check claude.ts authorship and my edits

step.text:
Done — the watchdog is in and verified against real hanging processes.

**It's an idle timeout, not a wall-clock cap.** That's the important design call: any output resets the clock, so a genuinely-working xhigh run that streams reasoning for 40 minutes is never touched. Only true silence kills. Default **10 minutes**, overridable per-provider via `idleTimeoutMs`.

Four changes in `src/runner/providers/cli.ts`:

1. **`bump()` on every stdout/stderr chunk** — restarts the idle timer. Also called **at spawn**, since the hang you actually hit happened *before the first byte*; a timer that only started on first output would have missed it entirely.
2. **SIGTERM → SIGKILL escalation** (`KILL_GRACE_MS = 5000`). A wedged process can ignore SIGTERM and leave `run.done` unresolved forever, blocking that thread's queue — the exact failure mode you were trying to close. This also fixes the manual `kill()` path, which previously only sent SIGTERM.
3. **Timers cleared on close and on spawn error**, and `unref()`'d so they never hold the process open.
4. **Distinct error message** — `"<Label> sent no output for 10m and was stopped."` — but a parsed error still wins, so a real message like the usage-limit one surfaces instead of a generic timeout.

**Verification** — 4 new tests in `tests/providers.integration.test.ts` (19 passed), driving `makeCliProvider` against real spawned processes:

- a process that never emits → rejects with the timeout message
- a process streaming for **5× the idle window** → completes fine, proving the clock resets rather than capping
- a parsed error → wins over the generic message
- user `kill()` → still reports `Stopped`, not a timeout

Escalation needed its own check since the 5s grace is too slow for the suite. I ran it as a throwaway against a process that traps SIGTERM: resolved at **5506ms** (500ms idle + 5000ms grace), confirming SIGKILL landed. Without it that process hangs indefinitely. Temp file removed.

Two caveats, both honest:

- **The suite still shows 2 failures, neither mine.** `agents` passes in isolation (load flake); `heartbeat` is the same websocket ping/pong failure I reproduced on a clean HEAD worktree last turn. I re-ran both in isolation specifically because this change touches spawn lifecycle — I didn't want to wave them through on last turn's result.
- **`tsc` is not clean right now**, but the one error is `claude.ts:20 — Cannot find name 'FileChange'`. That's another agent mid-write on a file-diff feature, missing an import; `git log` shows two syncs on that file since my work started. My files typecheck clean and all my edits survived their writes.

One thing I'd flag but didn't do: 10 minutes is a guess tuned to be safely generous. If Sol at xhigh ever thinks longer than that between events, it'd get killed mid-thought — worth watching once your usage limit lifts on the 25th, since that's also when the codex tool-call and reasoning paths get their first live exercise.

title: @Claude I want very beautiful icons for the "Chat", "Space", and "Docs" They sho…

text:
@Claude I want very beautiful icons for the "Chat", "Space", and "Docs"

They should be animated when shown, and slide/fade in when active. When the tab isn't active, no icon is shown. The animation should be real, but brief. A real SVG animation, not just a pulse or a spin. These need to be custom-made icons. Think like Discord, Mobbin, or Twitter icons animate when you click them

promptText:
@Claude I want very beautiful icons for the "Chat", "Space", and "Docs"

They should be animated when shown, and slide/fade in when active. When the tab isn't active, no icon is shown. The animation should be real, but brief. A real SVG animation, not just a pulse or a spin. These need to be custom-made icons. Think like Discord, Mobbin, or Twitter icons animate when you click them

step.text: I'll look at how tabs and icons currently work in the app before building these.

step.name: Bash
step.detail: Read design system memory and css

step.text: Design memory says "icons only from heroicons, never hand-rolled SVGs" — you're explicitly overriding that here, so I'll build custom ones and update that note after.
Now the keyframes:

Done — three custom icons in `src/renderer/src/components/TabIcon.tsx`, only rendered for the active tab.

**The animations** (all real SVG path drawing via `pathLength="1"` + `stroke-dashoffset`, not pulses):

- **Chat** — speech bubble strokes itself in one continuous 340ms pass starting at the top-left, tail last; then the three dots land one after another, 70ms apart, with a slight overshoot.
- **Space** — core dot pops, then two rotated orbit ellipses sweep out from it in sequence, then a satellite settles onto the outer ring.
- **Docs** — page outline draws, the corner folds over along its own 180ms path, then the three text lines rule themselves in left-to-right.

**Slide-in:** the wrapper `.tab-icon` animates `width: 0 → 20px` plus opacity and `translateX(-5px)`, so the icon opens its own slot and pushes the label right rather than popping on top of it. Total ≈560ms, front-loaded — brief. Reduced-motion collapses it all to 1ms.

Rendered the static end-states headlessly to check the geometry (`/tmp/icons.png` above) — shapes read correctly at size. Renderer typechecks clean; the one `tsc` error is pre-existing in `tests/claude-thinking.test.ts` from someone else's in-flight `fileChanges` export, not from this change. I didn't launch the app, so the motion timing itself is unverified in situ — worth a look on your end.
